coredata.dc:description: Sulfur ion (S2−) plays a significant and considerable role in many living organisms and ecosystems, while its abnormal content can pose a serious hazard to human health and ecological environment. Hence, it is extremely meaningful to construct a highly sensitive and selective analytical platform for S2− detection in complex microenvironment, particularly in biological systems. In this study, phosphomolybdic acid and L-Arg were utilized to prepare a new molybdenum doped carbon-dots nanozyme (Mo-CDs) with great peroxidase-like activity by one-step hydrothermal approach. In the presence of H2O2, Mo-CDs converted 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethyl benzidine (TMB) into blue oxTMB, but S2− strongly reduced the blue solution to colorless and then brown, which established significant selectivity toward S2−. Mo-CDs illustrated a wide linear range (2.5 μM–900 μM) and low detection limit (LOD = 76 nM) by ultraviolet and smartphone-assisted visualized colorimetric analysis. Especially, the smartphone-assisted analysis platform successfully realized quick, portable, sensitive and visible identification of S2− with high recovery (95.7–106.7 %) and excellent specificity in water samples. More importantly, Mo-CDs was developed to antibacterial applications based on good peroxidase-like activity. This research not only constructed a new and efficient carbon-dots nanozyme and a low-cost, portable, visual analysis platform for real-time detection of S2−, but also proposed a novel design strategy and methodology for exploiting multifunctional nanozyme detection tool with great practical application.

head.ce:title: Mo-doped carbon-dots nanozyme with peroxide-like activity for sensitive and selective smartphone-assisted colorimetric S2− ion detection and antibacterial application

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Scheme 1. Synthesis of Mo-CDs, colorimetric mechanism of smartphone-assisted dual-channel sulfur ion analysis platform.

Sulfur ion (S2−) plays a significant and considerable role in many living organisms and ecosystems, while its abnormal content can pose a serious hazard to human health and ecological environment. Hence, it is extremely meaningful to construct a highly sensitive and selective analytical platform for S2− detection in complex microenvironment, particularly in biological systems. In this study, phosphomolybdic acid and L-Arg were utilized to prepare a new molybdenum doped carbon-dots nanozyme (Mo-CDs) with great peroxidase-like activity by one-step hydrothermal approach. In the presence of H2O2, Mo-CDs converted 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethyl benzidine (TMB) into blue oxTMB, but S2− strongly reduced the blue solution to colorless and then brown, which established significant selectivity toward S2−. Mo-CDs illustrated a wide linear range (2.5 μM–900 μM) and low detection limit (LOD = 76 nM) by ultraviolet and smartphone-assisted visualized colorimetric analysis. Especially, the smartphone-assisted analysis platform successfully realized quick, portable, sensitive and visible identification of S2− with high recovery (95.7–106.7 %) and excellent specificity in water samples. More importantly, Mo-CDs was developed to antibacterial applications based on good peroxidase-like activity. This research not only constructed a new and efficient carbon-dots nanozyme and a low-cost, portable, visual analysis platform for real-time detection of S2−, but also proposed a novel design strategy and methodology for exploiting multifunctional nanozyme detection tool with great practical application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfide is a well-known hazardous contaminant, mostly generated and released during the manufacturing process of sulfur, sulfuric acid, dyestuff, cosmetic, biomass decomposition, petroleum extraction, papermaking, and related wastewater [1,2]. Sulfur ion (S2−), as one of the most important sulfides, plays a significant and considerable role in many living organisms and ecosystems [3], which can inhibit the activity of aquatic organisms by consuming oxygen in water [4–6]. However, abnormal content of S2− can bring a great threat to human health and the environment, which is the main chemical to produce acid rain and strongly associated with multitudinous diseases [7], exemplified by hypertension, hepatocirrhosis, gastrohelcosis, mucosal damage, glycuresis, Alzheimer's disease, and Down's syndrome [8–11]. Meanwhile, its molecular hydrogen sulfide plays a crucial role in physiological and pathological processes as a gas signal molecule [12]. Hence, it is extremely meaningful to construct a concise, quick, highly sensitive, and selective analytical platform for S2− detection in complex living organisms, which will indicate profound significance and potential clinical application for prevention and early diagnosis of S2− related disease (Scheme 1 ).

Due to its significant physiological and pathological effects, several scientific analytical techniques have been systematically constructed to determine S2−, including inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [13], electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [14], fluorescent probes [15], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [16], electrochemistry [17,18], fluorimetry [19–22], and chemiluminescence (CL) [23]. Nevertheless, traditional analytical methods illustrated multitudinous limitations, including weak sensitivity, low selectivity, high expense, time-consuming, complex analytical apparatus, and operation processes. Chai et al. suggested a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on carbon dots and two-dimensional porphyrin MOFs for actual-time and precise detection of sulfide using a single signal channel [24], which was easily disturbed by many factors such as probe concentration, excitation intensity, and environmental changes, thus increasing the detection error [25]. Consequently, it is essential to construct a multi-channel sensing platform for S2− detection, which will indicate far-reaching importance and utility for both ecological environments and human health.

Nanozymes, a nanomaterial with enzyme-like properties, are considered as promising colorimetric probes with extensive practicality in various fields such as medicine, food, chemical engineering, agriculture, and the environment. Nanozymes with various structures and compositions indicated different simulated enzyme functions, exemplified by activity similar to oxidase or peroxidase [26–30]. Generally, nanozymes with peroxidase-like activity are able to catalyze hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) [31]. Liu et al. developed a colorimetric sensor utilizing a 2D/2D heterojuncted nanozyme for detecting sulfide ions (S2−) [32]. Liao et al. reported a porous organic polymer (Fe-PPOP) based on iron porphyrins as an oxidase mimic for sulfide ion colorimetric detection [33]. The above research established a wide detection range, but low selectivity and accuracy provoked by single channel detection were still unresolved. Optical absorption and visualized colorimetry have attracted much more attention because of easy preparation, non-invasion, accessibility, dual-mode analysis with high sensitivity and selectivity, excellent compatibility, and accessibility for biosamples, which effectively avoided instrumental analysis error by double insurance and enhanced anti-interference ability in complex environments [34]. Therefore, the dual-mode analytic techniques demonstrated great promise and practical prospects for solving key scientific issues. Importantly, common artificial enzyme mimics indicated numerous shortcomings and drawbacks, including expensive manufacturing, complicated composition, maintenance costs, unstable performance, and serious cytotoxicity for biological systems [35]. Carbon-dots nanozymes exhibited outstanding advantages, exemplified by excellent water dispersibility, low toxicity, high stability, flexible composition, simple preparation, and modification [36–38], qualifying them as viable options for enzymatic uses, particularly in peroxidase (POD) mimics [39,40].

Few studies reported the detection of S2− by nanozyme materials. In this context, a straightforward one-step hydrothermal synthesis method was utilized to prepare a multifunctional Mo-doped carbon-dots nanozyme (Mo-CDs) by phosphomolybdic acid and L-Arg. Mo-CDs doped with transition metal Mo could transform H2O2 into O2 •−, demonstrating excellent peroxide-like activity, and specific binding ability with S2− to generate MoS (molybdenum-sulfur) bond, which ensured the accuracy and anti-interference in a complex sample environment. Through a smartphone-assisted colorimetric analysis platform, Mo-CDs exhibited remarkable superiority of rapid, portable, accurate visual monitoring and real time on-site detection of S2− in water samples without complicated equipment and instruments. Based on the above special characteristic, the ultraviolet and colorimetric dual-mode probe for sulfur ions and a novel bactericide with antibacterial activity have been developed and constructed (Scheme 1). The Mo-doped carbon-dots nanozyme demonstrated tremendous promise for precise determination of S2− and bacterial clearance in sewage.

All chemical reagents utilized in this experiment are analytical grade and unfiltered. H3PO4·12MoO3 (≥98 %), L-Arg (≥98 %), H2O2 (≥30 %), anhydrous CH3COONa (NaAc, ≥99 %), 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) (≥98 %), anhydrous CH3COOH (HAc, ≥99 %), Na2S (≥98 %), ethanol (≥99.5 %), Metal salts (MgCl2, CuSO4, CaCl2, Pb(NO3)2, MnCl2, NaF, Na2Ac, Na2CO3, KCl, NaH2PO4, ZnCl2, NaHCO3, NaNO3, NaCl, Na2SO4, BaCl2) are provided by Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China) and the purity is generally ≥98 %. Dulbecco Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) and LB AGAR (powder) were afforded by Sigma Aldrich (Shanghai, China). MilliQ water purification system (18.25 MΩ cm, Millipore Co., USA) provided all ultrapure water needed for sample dissolution.

H3PO4·12MoO3 (60 mg) and L-Arg (120 mg) were sonicated for 10 min to fully dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water, which was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave in an oven and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. The resulted solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min after cooling to room temperature in order to separate the precipitation and insoluble materials. The supernatant was extracted and obtained after a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The filtrate was dialyzed with a semipermeable membrane (MWCO: 500) for 4 h. Finally, the solid powder was acquired via vacuum freeze-drying.

The peroxidase activity of Mo-CDs was evaluated by a colorimetric method utilized TMB as a chromogenic substrate. Different concentrations of H2O2 (1.0–3.5 mM) or TMB (0.1–0.5 mM) were added into 150 μL Mo-CDs (32 mg mL−1) in acetate buffer (pH = 3.6). After stirring for 30 min at 20 °C, and the absorbance (A652 nm) of all samples was measured.

Different concentrations of S2− (2.5 μM–1 mM) were prepared for evaluating the selectivity and linear recognition of Mo-CDs. The high-concentration S2− detection platform was constituted with acetate buffer solution (pH = 3.6), 1.2 mg mL−1 Mo-CDs, and S2− at various concentrations. The low-concentration S2− analysis system consisted of 2.5 mM H2O2, 0.35 mM TMB, acetate buffer (pH = 3.6), and 1.2 mg mL−1 Mo-CDs. After incubating for 30 min at 20 °C, 100 μL S2− with different concentrations was added and achieved concentrations ranging from 2.5 to 900 μM, the absorbance change at 652 nm was recorded. In addition, storage solutions of different ions (Cu2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, K+, Na+, Cl−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, HCO3 −, H2PO4 −, F−) were prepared with ultrapure water to evaluate the selectivity of Mo-CDs.

EPR was used to investigate the enzymatic mechanism of Mo-CDs. Typically, 3730 μL acetate buffer (pH 3.6), 70 μL TMB substrate (20 mM), 50 μL H2O2 (200 mM), 150 μL Mo-CDs (32 mg mL−1) and 100 μL scavengers (tryptophan, isopropanol, or p-benzoquinone) with various concentrations were mixed thoroughly, and the absorption spectra were recorded after incubation for 30 min at 20 °C. The generation of superoxide anion (O2 •−) radicals was captured and confirmed by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) measurement utilizing 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin trapping agent [41]. To capture and detect O2 •−, 150 μL Mo-CDs solution (32 mg mL−1) and 50 μL H2O2 (0.2 M) were joined into 3800 μL acetate buffer (pH 3.6). The above solution (200 μL) was added into DMPO solution (100 mM, 200 μL) and uniformly mixed, which was sampled by capillary tube and measured under 300 W xenon lamp or in the darkness for 10 min, respectively [42].

The antimicrobial activity of Mo-CDs was evaluated in vitro by plate counting. E. coli and S. aureus were propagated in Luria-Bertani (LB) medium with shaking at 37 °C. The two bacteria were individually diluted to 1 × 107 CFU mL−1. The dispersed E. coli and H2O2 solution in different concentrations were added to acetate buffer. After incubation for 30 min, 100 µL of the mixed system was aspirated and inoculated onto agar plates. Finally, the concentration of H2O2 in this experiment was controlled at 1 mM.

To investigate the bactericidal efficacy of Mo-CDs at various concentrations, S. aureus and E. coli were randomly divided into two groups: the control group and Mo-CDs + H2O2 group. The reaction system consisted of bacteria (1 × 107 CFU mL−1), H2O2 (1 mM), and acetate buffer (PH = 5.6), and then 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1.0, and 1.2 mg mL−1 Mo-CDs were added, respectively. Significantly, the control group wasn't added any substance. Each sample (100 μL) was uniformly dispersed in LB solid medium by plate method and incubated at 37 °C for 14h. Colony-forming units (CFUs) were utilized to evaluate the antibacterial viability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological characterization of bacterial with Mo-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was utilized to examine the bacterial morphology after incubation with Mo-CDs. Firstly, the bacterial suspension samples treated with blank sample and Mo-CDs were centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min to collect precipitation. The bacterium was fixed by 4 % paraformaldehyde for 12 h under 4 °C. After centrifugation, PBS was utilized to wash the samples three times, which were progressively eluted with 10 %, 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, and 100 % ethanol, air-dried for 30 min, and treated with gold spray. Scanning electron microscopy was utilized to investigate bacterial morphology.

Mo-doped carbon dots (Mo-CDs) were prepared by hydrothermal kettle with H3PO4·12MoO3 and L-Arg as precursors. As displayed in Fig. S1, the optimal peroxidase-like activity of Mo-CDs was achieved through optimization of raw material proportions, reaction time, and temperature. The synthesized Mo-CDs illustrated good dispersibility and water solubility. Firstly, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was applied to examine the microstructure and morphology of Mo-CDs. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images demonstrated that Mo-CDs exhibited integral spherical particles and monodispersed well (Fig. 1 A).

In order to more clearly observe the detailed microstructure and crystal defects of Mo-CDs, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was utilized to characterize Mo-CDs microsphere. The HRTEM images (inset of Fig. 1A) exhibited a crystal structure with interlayer lattice spacing and fringes of 0.21 nm, which is near graphite carbon's (100) diffraction plane [43]. Mo-CDs displayed uniform and normal distribution with an average diameter of 3.34 nm (inset of Fig. 1A). By analyzing the absorption peaks of Mo-CDs through ultraviolet absorption spectroscopy, we can further understand the optical properties of Mo-CDs. In the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1B), the absorption peak at 244 nm was caused by the π → π* of the sp2 hybridization in the carbon core [39]. The graphite structure with a spacing of (100) is responsible for the conspicuous diffraction peak, which was observed in the powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of Mo-CDs at 22.5° (Fig. S2) [44].

FTIR spectroscopy displayed the characteristic functional group distribution on the surface of Mo-CDs (IR, Fig. 1C). The stretching and bending vibrations of CH were indicated by the peaks at 2944 and 2873 cm−1 [45]. Tensile vibrations of carbonyl (CO), amide II (NH), and amide III (CN) were attributable to the sharp and wide peaks at 1653, 1556, and 1435 cm−1, respectively. Broad bands centered at 3328 and 3354 cm−1 demonstrated the existence of single-bonded NH2 and OH, respectively. Therefore, FTIR demonstrated that Mo-CDs retained some characteristic functional groups of L-Arg [46]. Meanwhile, the peaks at 908 cm−1 were attributed to the characteristic absorption of MoO3 −, while 738, 545, and 4577 cm−1 characteristic peaks were attributed to the stretching and bending vibration of MoO [44].

The elemental compositions and functional groups of Mo-CDs before and after combination with S2− were further examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As exhibited in Fig. 1D, the four peaks were detected by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) at 232.5, 284.8, 401.0, and 532.2 eV, which belonged to Mo, C, N, and O, respectively [39]. Mo(IV) and Mo(VI) co-existed in sulfide sediments and sedimentary rocks with MoS (Molybdenum-Sulfur) bonds [47]. The specific binding of S2− and molybdenum ionic have been demonstrated by the characteristic peak value change before and after incubation with S2− (Fig. 1D). The XPS spectrum further revealed information about the chemical and compound states of all elements. The C 1s spectrum was divided into four components at 284.2, 284.8, 285.9, and 288.5 eV, which belonged to CC/CC, CN, CO, and CO, respectively (Fig. 1E) [48]. In the N 1 s spectra, the peaks at 398.3, 399.5, and 400.9 eV demonstrated the existence of CN [49], pyrrole N [50], and graphitic N [51], respectively (Fig. S3B). Three peaks at 530.0, 531.04, and 532.6 eV were exhibited in O 1 s XPS spectra (Fig. S3A), which stood for COH [52], CO [53], and OCO [54]. The mixed valence state of Mo4+ and Mo6+ in Mo-CDs was shown by the high-resolution XPS spectroscopic data of Mo 3d (Fig. 1F). The Mo4+3d5/2, Mo6+3d5/2, Mo4+3d3/2, and Mo6+3d3/2 peaks were located at 231.9, 232.3, 235.0, and 235.4 eV, respectively [55]. The mixed valence state may be attributed to the reducibility of L-Arg, which can reduce and transform Mo6+ in H3PO4·12MoO3 into Mo4+ during the hydrothermal synthesis process. The FTIR measurements and XPS investigation were consistent, which further confirmed the molybdenum doping was successfully implemented.

Significant application potential in biocatalysis was highlighted by the redox reaction between Mo in various valence states [56]. Thus, the biocatalytic activity of Mo-CDs was investigated and assessed. The simulated catalytic activity of Mo-CDs was investigated in the hybrid system constituted with sodium acetate solution, TMB, and H2O2, which was scanned by a UV–visible spectrophotometer after incubation for 30 min at 20 °C. As displayed in Fig. 2 A, Mo-CDs accelerated the oxidation of colorless TMB to blue oxidized TMB (oxTMB) in the presence of H2O2, which exhibited a distinctive absorption peak centered at 652 nm [31]. The enzyme activity was affected by various factors, such as pH, temperature, enzyme concentration, and reaction time [57]. In order to assess the influence of various Mo-CDs concentrations (0.4–1.4 mg mL−1), pH (2.5–6.0), reaction temperature (10–70 °C), and reaction duration (1.0–35 min), the mimic peroxidase (POD-like) activity was examined and investigated.

As illustrated from Fig. 2B, POD-like activity of Mo-CDs gradually increased and achieved its maximum value in the concentration range of 0.4–1.2 mg mL−1 and then descended to 1.2–1.4 mg mL−1. In addition, the temperature and pH also affect the catalytic activity of Mo-CDs. As displayed in Fig. 2C-–E–, Mo-CDs indicated the maximum enzymatic activity when the pH was raised to 3.6 during the 30-minute incubation period. Furthermore, the peroxidase-like activity in Mo-CDs progressively elevated from 10 °C to 20 °C but declined at higher temperatures. In conclusion, POD-like activity of Mo-CDs was the highest under the optimum experimental conditions of 1.2 mg mL−1 Mo-CDs, pH 3.6, 20 °C, and 30 min incubation. After 60 days of placement, the absorbance of Mo-CDs + TMB system maintained constant and remained stable, indicating that Mo-CDs possessed good photostability and strong chemical stability (Fig. 2F).

The steady-state kinetic study was performed under ideal circumstances utilizing TMB and H2O2 as substrates, and the simulated peroxidase activity of Mo-CDs was systematically explored and investigated. The Lambert-Beer rule was applied to calculate the starting velocities at various substrate concentrations. Ox-TMB illustrated a molar extinction value of 39,000 M−1 cm−1 at 652 nm. As exhibited by Fig. 3 A and B, the catalytic kinetic processes of two substrates with Mo-CDs indicated a typical Michaelis-Mending framework. By plotting the Lineweaver-Burk curve utilizing the Michaelis-Menten equation (Fig. 3C and D), Km and Vmax can thus be calculated to be 0.00412 mM and 3.32 × 10−8 M s−1 for TMB as substrate and 0.043 mM and 5.22 × 10−8 M s−1 for H2O2 as substrate, respectively. Mo-CDs possessed high affinity for TMB and H2O2, indicating strong peroxidase activity.

The catalytic mechanism of Mo-CDs was explored and studied by evaluating the production of typical reactive oxygen species (ROS) during the catalytic reaction. 1, 4-benzoquinone (PBQ), tryptophan (Try), and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) were utilized as scavengers of superoxide anion radicals (O2 •−), singlet oxygen (1O2), and hydroxyl radicals (•OH), respectively [58]. As displayed in Fig. 4 A, Mo-CDs still exhibited catalytic oxidation activity on TMB after the addition of isopropyl alcohol and tryptophan, which demonstrated that •OH and 1O2 didn’t exist in the catalytic process. However, p-benzoquinone can significantly reduce the relative activity and inhibit the catalytic reaction, which indicated that O2 •− is the primary reactive oxygen species generated in the H2O2 oxidation process catalyzed by Mo-CDs. In addition, the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analysis also provided direct evidence. The EPR spectrum was obtained by superoxide anion free radical scavenger (DMPO/O2 •−), which presented six different characteristic peaks and further directly demonstrated the generation of O2 •− in the catalytic process of Mo-CDs (Fig. 4B).

The catalytic mechanism is proposed and speculated based on these measurements and research results. Firstly, phosphomolybdic acid as a raw material may generate and introduce electron holes on the surface of Mo-CDs [59]. TMB was absorbed on the surface of carbon dots as an electron donor, which transferred the lone pair electrons from the amino group to Mo-CDs. Afterwards, the electron-rich Mo-CDs promoted electron migration to nearby H2O2 and produced large amounts of O2 •− [60,61], which oxidized TMB into colorless oxTMB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference research by Mo-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The selective measurement was devised and executed to assess the anti-interference capability of Mo-CDs in complex environmental samples. Sixteen different inorganic salt ions (Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, K+, Na+, Cl−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, HCO3 −, H2PO4 −, and F−) were examined for the interference effects of Mo-CDs. As exhibited in Fig. 5 A, when Cl−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, HCO3 −, H2PO4 −, NO3 −, F−, and S2− were added to Mo-CDs sensing systems, the blue color in the system became faded after addition of S2−, which could be observed by the naked eye (Fig. 5A). However, the color influence of other ions can be ignored, which laid the foundation for visual analysis of S2− by colorimetric method.

The Fig. 5B indicated the absorbance change of Mo-CDs at 652 nm following the addition of interference ions. The presence of S2− was exhibited to induce a considerable shift in ΔA, while the absorbance of the system at 652 nm didn't significantly fluctuate after incubation with other interfering ions, even though the concentration exceeded five times than S2−. Therefore, the above analysis demonstrated the excellent selectivity of Mo-CDs for ultraviolet analysis of S2−, which illustrated great potential and opportunity for actual sample application in complex environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sulfur ion detection based on Mo-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As a Lewis acid, sulfur ion (S2−) indicated a high electron-acquiring potential and reducibility [62], which will inhibit the oxidation of TMB and induce the blue solution to fade. The blue color gradually disappeared with increasing S2− concentration [33]. Consequently, the UV–vis spectra of Mo-CDs + TMB system was determined after the addition various concentration of S2− (2.5–25 μM). Fig. 6 B illustrated the linear curve (ΔA, A0 − A) for absorbance change at 652 nm with a concentration of S2−. The linear correlation between ΔA and S2− concentration is displayed as ΔA = 0.01273[S2−] + 0.00943 (R2 = 0.9977) with a range of 2.5 to 25 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) for S2− was determined to be 76 nM by Eq. 3.3σ/k. (k: slope; σ: standard deviation).

In addition, Mo ions can selectively react and bind with S2− to generate brown solutions, which was considered a crucial reason for the high selectivity of Mo-CDs toward S2−. As indicated in Fig. 6A, the color of the sensor system progressively turned from colorless to brown as the incremental concentration of S2−. As displayed in Fig. 6C, the analysis data were applied to fit the linear connection between absorbance and S2− concentration. The linear fitting equation between the concentration of S2− and A was: A = 9.987E − 4[S2−] + 0.06664 (R2 = 0.9975) when the concentration S2− fluctuated from 100 to 900 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 31.8 μM. Compared the detection performance with other methods (Table S1), Mo-CDs displayed significant superiority, including wider detection range and excellent sensitivity. Therefore, based on dual-mode colorimetric sensing particularity of Mo-CDs, the detection of S2− illustrated strong selectivity and anti-interference with wide detection range and low detection limit, which provided a practical and reliable method for S2− detection in complex environments, greatly improving the accuracy and reliability of real samples.

According to the results of the previous investigations, the visible color variations of Mo-CDs were strongly associated with the concentration of S2−. Nevertheless, the accuracy of visual analysis was limited and error-prone color distinction was assessed by the naked eye. RGB values represented the intensity of red, green, and blue light in a color, which combined these three colors with various ratios to produce different colors. The RGB values of the target image can be captured by a camera and identified using smartphone application software, which can then be combined with the concentration of the substance to establish an analytical model [63]. In this research, the RGB value was created to investigate and evaluate precise color variation by the F-Color Picker software of smartphone, which was utilized to visualize and quantify S2− concentration change.

As illustrated in Fig. 7 A, the blue color of Mo-CDs solution became colorless and transferred to brown with the incremental concentration of S2−. The RGB value of color variance was extracted and recorded by a smartphone-based color recognition app. The R, G, and B value were measured by F-Color Picker software of smartphone. The R value changed regularly with the system color variance. Therefore, the R-value was utilized as a standard to quantitatively measure the color change of S2− solution. The fitted linear curve was established between R value and S2− concentration, which illustrated excellent linear relationship. In Fig. 7B and C, the linear relationship of R value and S2− concentration was R = 1.009 [S2−] − 2.927 (R2 = 0.9927) at low linear range (5–200 μM), and the detection limit (LOD) was calculated as 13.02 μM from 3.3δ/k. Meanwhile, the fitting curve exhibited at high concentration ranges (200–900 μM) was R = 0.0455 [S2−] + 158.85 (R2 = 0.9855). The linear detection based on smartphone-assisted RGB value analysis established a solid foundation and great potential in visual quantitative analysis, which illustrated significant advantages of rapid, portable, visual, and real time on-site analysis without complex precision instruments.

To investigate the repeatability and potential application of quantitative detection by Mo-CDs in complicated environments, the standard concentration of S2− in pond and tap water was utilized to evaluate the analysis recovery rate and accuracy by Mo-CDs. As exhibited in Table 1 , the recovery rate and accuracy of S2− detection in actual samples is 95.7–106.7 %, RSD ≤ 6.9 %, respectively, which demonstrated that the sensing platform can be effectively utilized in environmental samples. The fitted linear curve was established between R value and S2− concentration. In summary, the smartphone-assisted Mo-CDs sensing platform offered a practical and straightforward method for S2− detection in real environment samples, which avoided intricate and practical deviations from huge analytical equipment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity of the Mo-CDs nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is the most common reactive oxygen species molecule in living organisms and a highly efficient chemical disinfectant with bactericidal ability [64]. Therefore, the antibacterial properties of H2O2 must first be evaluated before investigating the sterilization ability of Mo-CDs nanozyme. Fig. 8 A and C indicated that at least 0.1 M H2O2 was required to achieve excellent antibacterial properties. However, high concentrations of H2O2 exhibited strong oxidative damage and cytotoxicity. Thus, we speculated that Mo-CDs nanozyme with peroxidase activity can convert H2O2 into highly toxic reactive oxygen species (ROS). These free radicals can attack bacterial cell walls and intracellular components, leading to cell damage or death, thereby exerting antibacterial effects (Fig. 8F) [64].

As exhibited in Fig. 8B, E. coli and S. aureus were utilized as representative strains to evaluate the antibacterial effect of Mo-CDs nanozyme by plate counting method (MIC test) at different concentrations in vitro.

As displayed in Fig. 8D and E, 0.4 mg mL−1 Mo-CDs significantly reduced the viability of E. coli (about 35 %) and S. aureus (about 17 %) under the same concentration of H2O2 (1 mM). The survival rate of bacteria gradually decreased with the increased concentration of Mo-CDs. Especially, the bactericidal inhibition rates of S. aureus and E. coli under 1.2 mg mL−1 Mo-CDs were 99.36 % and 99.63 %, respectively, which demonstrated that the generation of O2 •− in the catalytic process of Mo-CDs attacked bacteria more aggressively than H2O2 alone. In conclusion, Mo-CDs revealed significant and enhanced inhibition against both bacteria in the presence of hydrogen peroxide, which is attributed to its excellent peroxidase-like activity.

To further decipher the antimicrobial behavior of Mo-CDs, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was applied to examine the morphological alterations of S. aureus and E. coli, which indicated perfect spherical and rod shape (Fig. 9 A and B), respectively. However, bacterial cell membrane structure significantly destroyed and damaged after treatment with Mo-CDs and H2O2, induced surface notable shrinkage and cracking (Fig. 9C and D), which attributed to the generation of O2 •− in the catalytic process by Mo-CDs. Morphological characterization further demonstrated Mo-CDs possessed great and powerful potential to be utilized in antibacterial applications.

In summary, multi-functional peroxidase-like carbon-dots nanozyme (Mo-CDs) was constructed and prepared by a concise and highly efficient hydrothermal method utilizing phosphomolybdic acid and L-Arg as precursors. Mo-CDs illustrated high peroxidase-like and antibacterial activity. Subsequently, a colorimetric and ultraviolet dual-mode detection platform was constructed utilizing Mo-CDs for specific quantification of S2− in tap and pond water with high accuracy. The UV absorbance and S2− exhibited excellent linear relationships in the high and low concentration ranges with a detection limit of 76 nM. In addition, a smartphone-assisted sensing platform was constructed for rapid, portable, visual, and real time on-site analysis of S2− without complex precision instruments. In summary, it is anticipated that the multifunctional nanozyme sensing platform will serve as a dependable and practical application in environmental governance, food security, and human health surveillance.
